question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unauthenticated /health/readiness endpoint to perform continuous polling and detect the exact moment the readiness check transitions from failing to passing, using this as a timing signal to know when a tampered Hashgraph history import has completed and the node is ready to serve falsified data?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any cryptographic verification of the GenesisBlockIdentifier.Hash in checkNetworkStatus(), meaning a node whose genesis block hash has been replaced with an attacker-controlled value will still pass the readiness check, and downstream Rosetta clients will accept the tampered genesis as legitimate Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not compare the CurrentBlockIdentifier against any expected checkpoint hash, allowing a node that has been fed a forked Hashgraph history (different block hashes at the same indices) to pass the readiness check and serve the forked history to Rosetta API consumers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NewHealthController() passes rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn() directly to the postgres health check, and if the DSN is logged or exposed in a health check error response, use the credentials to directly connect to the mirror node database and tamper with Hashgraph transaction history records?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check's internal rosettaClient uses http (not https) to connect to localhost, and by exploiting a server-side request forgery or local port redirect vulnerability, intercept the NetworkStatus response and inject a falsified CurrentBlockIdentifier, causing the health check to pass while the node serves tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/readiness is served without any authentication or IP allowlisting, to use the endpoint as a reconnaissance tool to determine the operational status of the Rosetta node and time an attack to inject tampered Hashgraph history during a maintenance window when readiness is already failing?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not validate the Peers list returned by NetworkStatus(), allowing a tampered node that has removed all legitimate Hashgraph network peers from its address book to pass the readiness check with an empty peer list, isolating it from the real network while serving falsified history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not enforce a minimum number of peers in the NetworkStatusResponse, allowing a node that has been network-isolated (zero peers) to pass the readiness check and serve tampered Hashgraph history without any peer-based cross-validation?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health controller's readiness checks run sequentially (postgresql first, then network), and by causing the postgresql check to consume its full 10-second timeout, delay the network check by 10 seconds, creating a window where the node is serving tampered Hashgraph history but the readiness probe has not yet evaluated the network state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not verify that the CurrentBlockTimestamp returned by NetworkStatus() is recent (within an expected range), allowing a node that has been rolled back to serve stale or tampered historical Hashgraph blocks while passing the readiness check?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the internal rosettaClient in checkNetworkStatus() has no configured request timeout beyond the health check's 10-second outer timeout, allowing a slow-response attack on the internal /network/list endpoint to hold the health check goroutine open for up to 10 seconds per request, enabling a DoS that prevents readiness detection of tampered history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health endpoints are registered in the same server.Router as the Rosetta API endpoints, meaning a vulnerability in the health endpoint routing (e.g., path traversal) could expose internal Rosetta API handlers that serve Hashgraph history data without the expected validation middleware?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not validate that the GenesisBlockIdentifier.Index is exactly 1 (or the expected genesis index), allowing a tampered node that has prepended fake blocks before the real genesis to pass the readiness check with a falsified genesis block index?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not compare the network identifier returned by NetworkList() against a hardcoded expected value, allowing a node that has been reconfigured to report a different blockchain/network name to pass the readiness check while serving history from a different (potentially tampered) Hashgraph network?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NewHealthController() does not validate rosettaConfig.Port before constructing the internal server URL, and if port is 0 or an unexpected value, the health check connects to an unintended service that returns a crafted NetworkStatus response with falsified Hashgraph block identifiers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check's network check calls the same /network/status endpoint that external Rosetta clients use, meaning any tampered data that passes the NetworkStatus handler's validation will also pass the health check, creating a false sense of integrity for the Hashgraph history being served?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not record or compare the GenesisBlockIdentifier.Hash across successive health check invocations, allowing an attacker who gradually replaces the genesis block hash in the database to go undetected, as each individual health check passes without noticing the hash has changed from the previous check?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unauthenticated /health/readiness endpoint to perform a binary search over time, determining the exact block height at which tampered Hashgraph history was injected by correlating readiness failures (caused by database inconsistencies) with specific time windows?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not validate the SubNetworkIdentifier field of the returned network identifier, allowing a tampered node that has changed its sub-network identity to pass the readiness check while serving history from a different shard or realm of the Hashgraph network?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the postgres health check uses sslmode=disable (as constructed by GetDsn()), meaning database traffic including Hashgraph history queries is unencrypted, and a network-adjacent attacker could perform a man-in-the-middle attack to inject tampered transaction records that pass the health check?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not enforce that CurrentBlockIdentifier.Index > GenesisBlockIdentifier.Index, allowing a tampered node where the current block has been rolled back to the genesis index to pass the readiness check while serving a truncated or falsified Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health check's internal HTTP client (client.NewConfiguration with nil http.Client) uses default Go HTTP client settings with no connection pooling limits, allowing a flood of GET /health/readiness requests to exhaust file descriptors via unclosed internal HTTP connections, crashing the health subsystem and masking tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/liveness always returns HTTP 200 (as it has no checks), to use liveness success as a false signal of node integrity, while the node is actually serving tampered Hashgraph history that only the readiness check (which they can suppress via DoS) would detect?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not validate the OldestBlockIdentifier field (if present in the NetworkStatusResponse), allowing a tampered node that has pruned early Hashgraph history to pass the readiness check while serving an incomplete or falsified historical record?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not verify the SyncStatus field of the NetworkStatusResponse, allowing a node that is actively syncing tampered Hashgraph history (and thus serving inconsistent data) to pass the readiness check before the tampered history is fully committed?
